title: Time to Study Graphs with Chef

content.problemStatement:
Consider directed graph which has **n + 2** layers numbered from left to right by integers from **0** up to **n + 1**. The leftmost (**0**) and the rightmost (**n + 1**) layers both contain **only one** vertex while every other layer contains exactly **m** vertices. Vertices are numbered independently in each layer by integers from **0** to **m - 1**. For each pair of vertices which are in the adjacent layers (**i** and **i + 1** for any **i (0 <= i <= n)**), there exists an edge. The vertex which is in the layer with smaller number is the initial vertex for such edge and the other one is the terminal vertex.



Based on a graph as described above, Chef added **k** more edges. Each edge connects two vertices which are in the different layers, no matter the adjacent layers or not. Also, each edge is directed from left to right (as well as all previously existing edges).



Chef is interested in the number of ways to get from the leftmost layer to the rightmost one. Two paths are considered different if there is, at least, one edge which belongs to exactly one path. However, they are allowed to traverse the same set of vertices. In that case, there should be a multiple edge in the graph. It is also possible if some edge added by Chef connects two adjacent layers.
constraints: For each added edge: 0 <= edge0 < edge2 <= n+1, 0 <= edge1, edge3 <= m-1
content.inputFormat: The first line contains three space-separated integers: n, m, and k. k lines follow, each describing one of the k additional edges and containing four space-separated integers: edge0, edge1, edge2, and edge3. The first two integers are for the initial vertex, and the other two are for the terminal vertex. Two integers each vertex is described by are the number of the layer (edge0 and edge2 respectively) and the number of the vertex in its layer (edge1 and edge3 respectively).
content.outputFormat: The number of ways to get from the leftmost layer to the rightmost one modulo 10^9 + 7.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The number of ways to get from the leftmost layer to the rightmost one is 19.
There are 3 ways to get from the leftmost layer to the rightmost one.
There are 4 ways to get from the leftmost layer to the rightmost one.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using dynamic programming. We create a 2D array dp of size (n+2) x m, where dp[i][j] represents the number of ways to reach the jth vertex in the ith layer. We initialize dp[0][0] to 1, as there is only one way to reach the first vertex in the first layer. Then, we iterate over the k additional edges and update the dp array accordingly. Finally, we calculate the number of ways to reach the last vertex in the last layer by summing up the values in the last layer.
solution.timeComplexity: O(k * m + n * m^2) per test case
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n * m) — the dominant structure is the 2D dp array
